behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior across directory depth. A unit test of path logic cannot catch non-recursive watch registration or OS-specific event delivery gaps. MOCK-HIDE applies: mocked watcher events can make subdirectory delivery look correct even when real watch setup misses nested paths. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text "placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed" requires validating real watch semantics, which disqualifies lower-level-only wiring tests.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory after startup, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is local decision logic (same path + time window) and does not inherently require OS/network/subprocess. Real failure is at function/object boundary (cache/key/time-window check). No LLM dependency, no multi-step agent pipeline. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text is specifically about "deduplication logic" and can be fully exercised with injected clock/time values and repeated requests.
behaviors.test_description: Test dedup component with a fake clock: submit path A at t0 (accepted), submit path A at t0+delta within window (discarded), submit path A after window expiry (accepted). Also verify different path is not discarded.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly references OS duplicate event emission. Exact failure appears at OS watcher boundary and event burst characteristics. In-process fakes can force idealized sequences and hide real duplicate patterns. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but explicit text "operating system emits multiple filesystem events" disqualifies lower-level-only module wiring as sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write + rename or chunked append), capture processing outputs/logs, and assert one and only one processing result for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real filesystem lifecycle events (delete/recreate) and long-running watcher recovery behavior. This is OS primitive interaction plus daemon continuity, not just local orchestration. MOCK-HIDE: mocked watcher callbacks can bypass real unwatch/rebind edge cases. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text "directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" directly requires real FS event/state transitions.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watch path, delete the watched directory while running, recreate it at same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on interaction between real arrival timing/events and filesystem timestamp metadata resolution. A mocked pipeline can hide race and tie-order issues seen under real FS/event scheduling. Integration can validate sorter logic, but minimum to catch real boundary failures in "arrive at the same time" conditions is System.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on real directories, create multiple files in rapid succession (or concurrently), record observed processing order from outputs/logs, compare against actual file creation timestamps read from filesystem metadata, and assert ordering matches spec.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure depends on real write-in-progress behavior and watcher timing. Correctness hinges on OS/filesystem semantics (partial writes, flush/close timing) that in-process fakes often do not replicate. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text "still being written" and "arrival event fires" requires real file I/O timing conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, begin writing a large file in chunks with delays to the watched directory, verify no processing occurs before writer closes/completes, then verify exactly one processing run happens after completion with full file content.
